prompt:
Estás en un directorio que contiene una tarea de programación. Lee los archivos disponibles y resuelve la tarea de la forma más completa posible.

Puedes crear, editar o eliminar archivos dentro de este directorio. Si el enunciado no especifica un lenguaje, framework o estructura de proyecto, elige una alternativa pragmática.

Al terminar, deja instrucciones claras para ejecutar o revisar tu solución.

No hagas preguntas de seguimiento. Adopta supuestos razonables y documéntalos.

content.text:
**Inspecting workspace**

I need to take a closer look at my workspace. There might be things I can improve or rearrange to make it more efficient. I'm curious about how the layout affects my productivity and whether there are items I can remove or repurpose. It can be a bit overwhelming at times, but I’m excited to see how small changes can make a big difference. Let’s see what I can find and adjust!

turns.toolResult:
CC3301_otoño_2024_tarea4.md
Makefile
README.md
bench.c
consultar.c
defs.dicc
prof.ref-aarch64
prof.ref-x86_64
pss.c
pss.h
revisar.c
test-consultar.sh

==========================================================
Esta es la documentación para compilar y ejecutar su tarea
==========================================================

Se está ejecutando el comando: less README.txt

***************************
*** Para salir: tecla q ***
***************************

Para avanzar a una nueva página: tecla <page down>
Para retroceder a la página anterior: tecla <page up>
Para avanzar una sola línea: tecla <enter>
Para buscar un texto: tecla / seguido del texto (/...texto...)
         por ejemplo: /ddd

-----------------------------------------------

Ud. debe programar su solución en el archivo consultar.c.

Debe probar su tarea bajo Debian 12 nativo o virtualizado.
Queda excluido WSL 1 para hacer las pruebas.  Sí puede usar WSL 2.
Estos son los requerimientos para aprobar su tarea:

+ make run-san debe felicitarlo y no reportar ningún problema como
  por ejemplo memory leaks.
+ make run-g debe felicitarlo.
+ make run debe felicitarlo.

Invoque el comando make zip para ejecutar todos los tests y generar un
archivo consultar.zip que contiene consultar.c, con su solución,
y resultados.txt, con la salida de make run, make run-g y make run-san.

Para ejecutar la solución de referencia, ejecute estos comandos:

ARCH=$(arch)
bash test-consultar.sh prof.ref-$ARCH

-----------------------------------------------

Para depurar use: make ddd

Recuerde que para ejecutar el programa debe ingresar run en el panel de
comandos de ddd, especificando los parámetros.  Por ejemplo:

run defs.dicc bolsillo

Video con ejemplos de uso de ddd: https://youtu.be/FtHZy7UkTT4
Use los archivos de ejemplo incluidos localmente.

-----------------------------------------------

Producto esperado

Ejecute: make zip

Genere el archivo consultar.zip

Verifique el archivo generado ejecutando nuevamente los tests antes de considerarlo listo.

-----------------------------------------------

Limpieza de archivos

make clean

Hace limpieza borrando todos los archivos que se pueden volver
a reconstruir a partir de los fuentes: *.o binarios etc.

-----------------------------------------------

Acerca del comando make

El comando make sirve para automatizar el proceso de compilación asegurando
recompilar el archivo binario ejecutable cuando cambió uno de los archivos
fuentes de los cuales depende.

A veces es útil usar make con la opción -n para que solo muestre
exactamente qué comandos va a ejecutar, sin ejecutarlos de verdad.
Por ejemplo:

   make -n ddd

También es útil usar make con la opción -B para forzar la recompilación
de los fuentes a pesar de que no han cambiado desde la última compilación.
Por ejemplo:

   make -B run

CC3301 Programación de Software de Sistemas – Semestre Otoño 2024 – Tarea 4 �

Un   diccionario   persistente   se   almacena   en   un   archivo   en   formato
binario.     El   diccionario   se   representa   mediante   un   árbol   binario   de
búsqueda en donde cada nodo del árbol utiliza un área contigua en el
archivo con los siguientes campos:

•

•

•

•

izq:   Un   entero   de   4   bytes   (en   formato   binario  little   endian)
correspondiente al desplazamiento a partir del cual se encuentra
la   raíz   del   subárbol   izquierdo.     Es   -1   si   no   posee   subárbol
izquierdo.  El desplazamiento 0 corresponde a la raíz del árbol.
der: Como izq pero para el subárbol derecho.
tam_llave:   Un   entero   de   2   bytes   (en   formato   binario  little
endian)   correspondiente   al   tamaño   en   caracteres   de   la   llave
almacenada en el nodo.
tam_valor:   Un   entero   de   2   bytes   (en   formato   binario  little
endian)   correspondiente   al   tamaño   en   caracteres   del   valor
asociado a la llave de ese nodo.
llave: Secuencia de  tam_llave  bytes correspondiente a la llave
(codificada en ascii).  No incluye terminación de string.
valor:   Secuencia   de  tam_valor  bytes   correspondiente   al   valor
(codificado en ascii).  No incluye terminación de string.
Un   ejemplo   de   diccionario   es   el   archivo  defs.dicc  incluido   en   los
archivos adjuntos.   No es legible al examinarlo con un editor de texto
porque es  un archivo  binario, no de texto.    Aún así  podrá distinguir
llaves   y   valores,   pero   no   desplazamientos   ni   tamaños.     El   programa
revisar.c incluido verifica la consistencia de un diccionario y muestra su
contenido.   Estudie este programa, ya que le servirá para resolver esta
tarea.  Compile y ejecute revisar.c con este comando:

•

•

make revisar

En esta tarea se pide programar el comando  ./consultar.bin  que recibe
como parámetros el archivo que guarda un diccionario y una llave.  El
comando busca eficientemente la llave en el diccionario y entrega en la
salida   estándar   el   valor   asociado   a   esa   llave.     Debe   programar   este
comando   en   el   archivo  consultar.c.     Compile   y   ejecute   con   estos
comandos:

make consultar.bin
./consultar.bin   defs.dicc   perro

La salida debe ser: mamifero domestico con olfato muy fino, inteligente
y leal

El comando ./prof.ref-$(arch) es un programa que hace exactamente lo
mismo   que   se   pide   para   el   comando  ./consultar.bin.     Ejecútelo   por
ejemplo con el comando:

./prof.ref-$(arch)   defs.dicc   perro

El test de prueba verifica que su solución haga exactamente lo mismo
que  ./prof.ref-$(arch).   En particular revisa que la salida  estándar, la
salida estándar de errores y el código de retorno sean iguales.
Observe que en esta tarea Ud. debe programar la función  main.  En
las tareas anteriores la función  main  estaba dada en el archivo con los
tests de prueba de su solución.
Requerimientos:

• Debe   ser   eficiente:   Minimice   la   cantidad   de   nodos   que   se
necesita   leer   para  encontrar  la   llave,   aprovechando   que   es   un
árbol binario de búsqueda.   No puede leer secuencialmente el
archivo.     Use  fseek  para   posicionarse   directamente   en   el
desplazamientos que está en los campos  izq  o  der.   La raíz del
árbol está en la posición 0.
Si no puede leer el diccionario, debe diagnosticar el error con la
función perror.
Si la llave no existe o no se recibe la cantidad de parámetros
correcta,   debe   diagnosticar   el   error   en   la   salida   estándar   de
errores, con el mismo mensaje que ./prof.ref-$(arch).

•

•

Instrucciones
Use los archivos incluidos en este directorio.   Ejecute el comando
make  sin   parámetros   en   el   directorio  T4  para   recibir   instrucciones
adicionales.

Producto esperado
Genere el archivo `consultar.zip` con `make zip`. Verifique el archivo generado ejecutando nuevamente los tests antes de considerarlo listo.

# Para revisar las opciones de compilacion y ejecucion,
# ingrese en el terminal el comando: make
#
#

PROB=consultar

SRCS= $(PROB).c
HDRS= 

SHELL=bash -o pipefail

INCLUDE=
CFLAGS=-Wall -Werror -pedantic -std=c18 -Wno-unused-function $(INCLUDE)
LDLIBS=-lm
ARCH=$(shell arch)

MAK=make --no-print-directory

readme:
	@less README.txt

revisar:
	$(MAK) PROB=revisar SRCS="revisar.c pss.c" HDRS=pss.h revisar.bin
	@echo
	./revisar.bin defs.dicc
	@echo
	@echo "Estudie este programa ejecutandolo bajo ddd con este comando:"
	@echo "make revisar.ddd"

revisar.ddd:
	$(MAK) PROB=revisar SRCS="revisar.c pss.c" HDRS=pss.h revisar.bin-g
	ddd revisar.bin-g &
	@echo "Coloque un breakpoint con: b main"
	@echo "Ejecute el programa con: r defs.dicc"
	@echo "Ejecute paso a paso con el boton next"

revisar.san:
	$(MAK) PROB=revisar SRCS="revisar.c pss.c" HDRS=pss.h revisar.bin-san
	./revisar.bin-san defs.dicc

bench:
	$(MAK) PROB=bench bench.bin-san

$(PROB).bin $(PROB).bin-g $(PROB).bin-san: $(SRCS) $(HDRS)

run-san: $(PROB).bin-san
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs" ; echo "Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco con el comando expand!" ; echo "No olvide revisar su indentacion"; false ; else true; fi
	bash test-$(PROB).sh $(PROB).bin-san

run-g: $(PROB).bin-g
	bash test-$(PROB).sh $(PROB).bin-g

prof.ref: $(PROB).bin
	cp $(PROB).bin prof.ref-$(ARCH)
	strip prof.ref-$(ARCH)

run: $(PROB).bin
	bash test-$(PROB).sh $(PROB).bin
	$(MAK) PROB=bench bench.bin
	./bench.bin ./prof.ref-$(ARCH) ./$(PROB).bin

ddd: $(PROB).ddd

ddd-san: $(PROB).ddd-san

zip:
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs.  Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco!" ; false ; else true; fi
	@rm -f resultados.txt $(PROB).zip
	@echo "Sistema operativo utilizado" > resultados.txt
	@uname -a >> resultados.txt
	@cat resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-san ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-san | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-g ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-g | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== zip ====
	zip -r $(PROB).zip resultados.txt $(PROB).c
	@echo "Genere el archivo $(PROB).zip"
	@echo "Descomprima el archivo generado y revise"
	@echo "que esta su ultima version de $(PROB).c"

%.bin: %.c
	gcc -O -DOPT=1 $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-g: %.c
	gcc -g $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-san: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -fsanitize=address -fsanitize=undefined $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.ddd: %.bin-g
	ddd $(*F).bin-g &

%.ddd-san: %.bin-san
	ddd $(*F).bin-san &

clean:
	rm -rf *.o *.log *.bin* core $(PROB).zip resultados.txt bin obj err-ref.txt err.txt raw-std-ref.txt raw-std.txt std-ref.txt std.txt big.dicc res.txt

#!/usr/bin/bash

trap "rm -rf /tmp/defs.dicc" EXIT
ARCH=$(arch)
CONSULTAR=./$1
chmod +x $CONSULTAR

test() {
  DICC=$1
  LLAVE=$2
  EXTRA=$3
  echo "Ejecutando: $CONSULTAR $DICC $LLAVE $EXTRA"
  ( $CONSULTAR $DICC $LLAVE $EXTRA > raw-std.txt ) >& err.txt
  RC=$?
  cat raw-std.txt | xargs > std.txt
  ( ./prof.ref-$ARCH $DICC $LLAVE $EXTRA > raw-std-ref.txt ) >& err-ref.txt
  REFRC=$?
  cat raw-std-ref.txt | xargs > std-ref.txt

  cmp err.txt err-ref.txt
  if [ $? -ne 0 ]
  then
    echo "*** Salida estandar de errores incorrecta ***"
    echo "Su solucion entrega en la salida estandar de errores:"
    echo "============================================================"
    cat err.txt
    echo "============================================================"
    echo "Debio ser:"
    echo "============================================================"
    cat err-ref.txt
    echo "============================================================"
    echo "Si la salida parece ser igual, instale xxdiff con:"
    echo "    sudo apt-get install xxdiff"
    echo "y luego compare los caracteres invisibles con:"
    echo "xxdiff err.txt err-ref.txt"
    echo "Para depurar lance ddd con: make ddd"
    echo "En la ventana de ddd, coloque un breakpoint con: b main"
    echo "Ejecute con: run defs.dicc $LLAVE $EXTRA"
    echo "Luego ejecute paso a paso con el boton next"
    exit 1
  fi
  cat err.txt

  cmp std.txt std-ref.txt
  if [ $? -ne 0 ]
  then
    echo "*** Salida estandar incorrecta ***"
    echo "Su solucion entrega en la salida estandar:"
    echo "============================================================"
    cat std.txt
    echo "============================================================"
    echo "Debio ser:"
    echo "============================================================"
    cat std-ref.txt
    echo "============================================================"
    echo "Si la salida parece ser igual, instale xxdiff con:"
    echo "    sudo apt-get install xxdiff"
    echo "y luego compare los caracteres invisibles con:"
    echo "xxdiff std.txt std-ref.txt"
    echo "Para depurar lance ddd con: make ddd"
    echo "En la ventana de ddd, coloque un breakpoint con: b main"
    echo "Ejecute con: run defs.dicc $LLAVE $EXTRA"
    echo "Luego ejecute paso a paso con el boton next"
    exit 1
  fi
  cat raw-std.txt

  if [ "$RC" == 0 ]
  then
    if [ "$REFRC" != 0 ]
    then
      echo "El codigo de retorno es incorrectamente 0.  Debio ser $REFRC"
      echo "Es el valor que retorna la funcion main, o el parametro de exit"
      exit 1
    fi
  fi
  if [ "$RC" != 0 ]
  then
    if [ "$REFRC" == 0 ]
    then
      echo "El codigo de retorno es incorrectamente $RC.  Debio ser 0"
      echo "Es el valor que retorna la funcion main, o el parametro de exit"
      exit 1
    fi
  fi
  if [ "$RC" != 0 ]
  then
    echo "Bien.  Se diagnostico correctamente el error."
  fi
}

echo "-----------------------------------------------------------"
echo "Tests unitarios"
test defs.dicc embarcacion
test defs.dicc casa
test defs.dicc lluvia
test defs.dicc alimento
test defs.dicc celular
test defs.dicc canario
test defs.dicc palacio
test defs.dicc bolsillo
test defs.dicc correr
test defs.dicc nadar
test defs.dicc posada
test defs.dicc taladro
test defs.dicc perro
test defs.dicc techo

echo "-----------------------------------------------------------"
echo "Test con llave inexistente"
test defs.dicc vaca

echo "-----------------------------------------------------------"
echo "Test con un diccionario inexistente"
test nodefs.dicc bolsillo
echo "-----------------------------------------------------------"
echo "Test con diccionario sin permiso de lectura"
cp defs.dicc /tmp/
chmod -r /tmp/defs.dicc
test /tmp/defs.dicc litigio
rm -f /tmp/defs.dicc

echo "-----------------------------------------------------------"
echo "Test de uso incorrecto de parametros"
test defs.dicc perro "mamifero domestico con olfato muy fino, inteligente y leal"
test defs.dicc

echo
echo "Felicitaciones: Aprobo todos los tests"

I'm looking to implement an efficient binary search on a file, which means I need to determine the exact output and error messages. It might be helpful to run reference tests on various cases to see how it behaves. I wonder if binary actually exists; maybe I should inspect file sizes or even run it directly? I think using a specific architecture will help in this process, so let's check that out too!

content.text:
**Considering implementation details**

I’m focused on a robust approach here. I think it's important to seek offsets directly in the left and right nodes. Given the variable key/value lengths, I need to know the exact size to calculate the next node's position after reading the current one. First, I plan to read the header followed by the key and value, then search accordingly. For binary search, I’ll compare at the node and then seek to the subtree offsets. Also, I need to consider using a long for file position functions.

**Analyzing file handling and testing**

I need to replace a whole file and check out test-consultar.sh for any extra behaviors. It looks like I'll use xargs on the output to handle trailing newlines properly, ensuring the command output is clean, with no extra spaces or lines. The test normalizes whitespace, which is essential. I must compare errors raw and ensure exact spacing for stdout and stderr messages. I'll also handle any file read errors due to permissions properly.

**Considering reading functions and architecture**

I'm thinking about using manual reading functions for better clarity and robustness. Since the requirement specifies x86_64, I wonder if I can run it on aarch64 as well. I want to ensure our code compiles on both architectures, which means proper endian handling is crucial. It might be smart to write functions for decoding little-endian bytes, possibly align struct reading, and use helper functions to read and decode fields individually. Adapting to the architecture as necessary will help.

**Implementing search logic**

I could start at pos=0 and loop through the structure. First, I'll use fseek to set the position and read the node header, which includes left, right, tam_llave, and tam_valor. Then, I need to allocate key and value buffers based on these sizes. After reading the key and value bytes, I'll compare the key to argv[2]. If they match, I’ll print the value and exit. For searching, if the key is less than the current, I’ll check left and adjust pos accordingly while managing memory efficiently.

**Clarifying error handling for the task**

I need to address error handling if I can't read the dictionary and consider printing an error message. It seems like the raw output when a key is missing might include a newline, so I’ll add that to my message. 

Also, the program usage needs to mention the exact name "consultar.bin," and I should ensure that if there are too many arguments, it shows the same usage message. Lastly, I need to manage memory properly to avoid leaks and ensure I exit with an error code when necessary.

**Improving code clarity**

I want to ensure I'm writing clean code, so I'm considering whether to use `ftell`. While it's not strictly necessary, if I'm using `fseek` to navigate left or right, I need to track absolute positions from the start of the file. Current file positions are integers from the root, which is at zero, so it seems like I can just use `fseek(dicc, pos, SEEK_SET)`. I think I need to use `long` for position tracking, like `long pos = 0;`, and I'll also need to handle very large values when comparing child offsets, which are `int32_t`.

**Considering efficient data searching**

I’m pondering how to read keys in a tree structure to minimize unnecessary value reads. To navigate to children, I don’t need the value, but to compare I do need the key. If I find a matching key, I'll then need the value, but if it doesn’t match, it makes sense to skip reading the value entirely to be more efficient. I’m thinking I could also use a function to move to the next node after evaluating each key.

**Evaluating data reading efficiency**

I’m considering how to navigate child positions directly without needing the value first. When I find the key, I’ll have to read the corresponding value bytes, which is straightforward. I wonder if `tam_valor` can be zero; could I allocate one byte and read zero bytes? That could work out nicely. I should also ensure the key in a file has the right length to match the target string, using `strcmp` or `strncmp` for comparison afterward.

**Handling read errors**

I need to decide whether to use `perror(nom)` or just `ferror`. If I reach EOF, it might indicate file corruption, which I haven't fully tested yet. I could write a check like `if (fread(...) != 1) { perror(nom); exit(1); }`, but EOF doesn’t set `errno`, leading to a misleading "Success" error message. It seems wiser to standardize failure handling, possibly with `fprintf(stderr, "%s: lectura incompleta\n", nom);`, but I should stick to the requirement of using `perror` for dictionary read errors.

**Considering output processing**

I’m thinking about whether I need to make sure the output is flushed correctly. Using printf seems fine, but I need to consider that for replacing the file entirely, it might be important to ensure everything is written as expected. It feels like a minor detail, but it matters for clarity and functionality. I’m going to double-check my approach to make sure I get it right.

content.text:
**Evaluating value truncation**

I’m noticing an interesting issue with a palacio value being truncated. It seems like our assumptions about reading lengths might be off. There’s a suggestion that an extra "a" might cause an output mismatch. I should inspect the node structure in the file to determine whether there are trailing NULs involved. It raises questions about how we're comparing keys and reading the `tam_valor`.  I’m starting to wonder if `tam_valor` is not being read correctly.

**Investigating node reading issues**

I think we might not be at the correct node or are misreading offsets due to the header layout, which could differ because of additional padding. I should inspect the file structure using the revisar tool. The `NodoArch` structure reads the header as 12 bytes, and our reading should align with that. The value truncation is puzzling. It might happen if `tam_valor` in the file is 49, while the string length is 50. I’m concerned that reading issues could lead to misinterpretation of data.

**Reassessing value reading**

If `tam_valor` is smaller than the actual string length, we might be omitting the last byte. The reference output shows an extra 'a,' which could indicate that their binary format uses `tam_valor` as the length excluding the last character. That doesn’t quite add up since our output is shorter. Maybe we’re misreading nodes or making incorrect comparisons. If the reference output is longer, it might suggest that we need to check the stored `tam_valor` in the file.

**Inspecting file details**

I'm questioning whether the `tam_valor` has been signed short or if it might overflow, though that seems unlikely. I think I should check the actual file around "palacio." Using tools like hexdump could help here. I might search for specific strings in the file too. I'll run a command with `strings -n 5 defs.dicc` to look for "palacio" and one line before and after to get context. That should help clarify things!

content.text:
**Inspecting string output**

I think there might be an issue with the strings not showing the final value, possibly because it's not printable. So, I need to inspect the reference with `revisar.bin`. Maybe this reference prints out the palacio value. I’m considering running the reference or using a local revisa tool to check what’s going on. There’s definitely something to figure out here!

content.text:
**Inspecting code discrepancies**

I’m thinking that "visar" might not be the same as "consult." It seems like I need to emulate the reference binary exactly, even though it might not align with "revisar." I should inspect the code on "palacio" after checking if the file's value has any extra characters, but it looks like "revisión" is truncating due to newlines or spaces. So, I’ll compare the raw bytes using the `xxd` command from the reference output.

content.text:
**Analyzing Bash Redirection**

I'm considering how the command `>& err.txt` in bash operates, specifically redirecting both stdout and stderr from a subshell into `err.txt`. So when I see `( command > raw-std.txt ) >& err.txt`, I'm thinking that `> raw-std.txt` is likely recording within the subshell. Then I'm puzzled about why `diff` shows an extra "a." Maybe it has to do with trailing spaces or line merging when using `xargs.` If my output ends with a space, that can mess things up! I’ll re-run things manually, especially if it failed during automated tests.

**Examining Test Failures**

I'm trying to figure out how to replicate the issue with the script steps. It's vital to know what the test expects from our file. I wonder if there’s a problem in reading `palacio` when running via `prof.ref`. Then there's the shell command `cmp std.txt std-ref.txt`, which should compare the outputs from `xargs`. If both outputs are identical, it shouldn't fail. But since it did, I’m thinking the output from the reference binary might be getting influenced by our binary somehow.

**Identifying command issues**

I’m reflecting on whether the 'a' in the diff could be due to the argument `palacio` being extra. The test line I'm using is `Ejecutando: ./consultar.bin defs.dicc palacio `. The trailing space seems to come from an empty EXTRA. I wonder if `xargs` is incorrectly interpreting EOF from `raw-std-ref.txt`. I'm realizing the `cmp` failure might not be direct output due to running commands with `multi_tool_use.parallel`, causing race conditions with shared temp files. I need to rerun tests sequentially to avoid this interference.

arguments.command: cd /workspace && make clean >/dev/null 2>&1 || true && make run

I need to run the "run-g" with sequential processing instead of parallel to fix weird concurrency issues. I'm also noticing that the output from "run-g" shows some bizarre mismatches in the test script, which could be due to the concurrency errors. To address this, it's a good idea to rerun "run-g" alone and then also run "run-san" by itself to isolate the issues. Let’s see what happens!

content.text:
**Evaluating zip command necessity**

I realize the zip command is missing. It seems like there’s no need to worry about it, though I could mention its absence. The task's instructions are clear, and generating a zip file may not be required. I could use Python's zipfile module to create the zip if necessary, but relying on an absent command seems unproductive. The instructions suggest generating a consulting zip, so maybe I should consider creating it manually despite potential failures.

**Creating the necessary files**

To be on the safe side, I can create both consultar.zip and resultados.txt myself. Since the make zip command failed due to its absence, manually creating the zip file makes sense. I might need to generate results.txt with my outputs, but I’m not entirely sure if that’s necessary—perhaps the evaluation only checks code. Since the task says to leave the solution, I’ll proceed with manually generating the zip file using a Python script, including results.txt and consultar.c.

arguments.command:
cd /workspace && python3 - <<'PY'
import zipfile
with zipfile.ZipFile('consultar.zip', 'w', compression=zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as z:
    z.write('resultados.txt')
    z.write('consultar.c')
print('created consultar.zip')
PY

**Considering running instructions**

I should mention how to run the program, like using `make run`, `make run-g`, or `make run-san`, since the user asked for instructions. It's important to note that `make zip` requires `zip` to be installed, but that might not be available in our environment. I’m wondering if I could create a zip using Node and its builtin `zlib` alongside some manual implementation, though that seems a bit complex. We may need to focus on what evaluation expects instead of creating `consultar.zip`.

**Creating a ZIP file**

I’m looking to generate a ZIP file named `consultar.zip`, and it might be better to create it manually if possible. Without the ZIP utility, I think I can use a Node.js script to create a valid ZIP file using raw buffers. I have enough time for this! I need to ensure the `resultados.txt` file exists and include it along with `consultar.c`. I should also implement CRC32 in JavaScript and consider setting DOS date/time to zero. Let’s get started!

**Building a minimal ZIP file**

I'm planning to create a minimal ZIP file with two stored entries since the files are small. First, I need to write a script using Buffer to handle the file data and build the local headers. I’ll include the local file header signature and required fields like version, flags, compression, CRC32, sizes, and filename info. 

Next, I’ll build the central directory header and the End of Central Directory (EOCD) structure. It feels like a lot, but I'm ready to tackle it!
